steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank must supply table evidence proving that both alkanes AND dimethyl sulfides were present in the pulverized sample before the methanol-only (third) extraction. Because each rinse pulls out whatever it can dissolve, detecting a compound in any extract confirms it was in the original material.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘the third extract in the sequence contains dimethyl sulfides and the fourth contains alkanes’: Extract 3 (methanol-only) shows dimethyl sulfides, and extract 4 shows alkanes. Both compounds are confirmed present in the original sample. This satisfies both halves of the claim. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘the second extract in the sequence contains alkanes but no dimethyl sulfides’: This confirms alkanes but says nothing about dimethyl sulfides appearing anywhere. It cannot prove both compounds were present. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘three of the four extracts contain alkanes but only one of the four contains dimethyl sulfides’: This describes frequency counts rather than pointing to specific detections. It also does not directly state in which extract dimethyl sulfides appear, making it weaker as evidence and failing to confirm the before-methanol timing. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘the fourth extract in the sequence contains alkanes but no dimethyl sulfides’: Confirms alkanes in extract 4 only; provides no evidence for dimethyl sulfides at all. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
